steps.caption_md: `insert("an")`: walk from the root, creating a child node for each new letter (`a`, then `n`). No node existed yet, so both are created. The last node, `n`, gets `is_word = True`.
`insert("ant")`: the path `a → n` already exists, so it's reused — only a new `t` child is created off of `n`. The `n` node keeps `is_word = True` from before; now `t` also gets `is_word = True`.
`search("an")` walks `a → n` and stops. The path exists — but does that alone mean `"an"` is a stored word?

predict.prompt_md: `search("an")` has walked to the `n` node, which also has a child `t` (because `"ant"` is stored). What does `search` return, and why?

choices.label_md: `True` — the `n` node's `is_word` flag is `True`, regardless of its children.
`False` — because `n` has a child (`t`), so `"an"` is only a prefix, not a full word.
`True`, but only because `"an"` happens to be a prefix of the longer word `"ant"`.

predict.explanation_md: `search` never looks at whether a node has children — it only checks the final node's `is_word` flag. `n.is_word` was set to `True` back when `"an"` was inserted, so `search("an")` returns `True` regardless of `"ant"` sharing the same path.

steps.caption_md: `starts_with("any")` walks `a → n` fine, then looks for a child `y` on the `n` node. There isn't one, so `_walk` returns `None` and `starts_with` reports `False` — no rescanning of `"an"` or `"ant"` needed, just one failed dict lookup.
